turns.content: I am going to need a moment to express something before I ask for your help, because I am currently exercising considerable restraint and it needs somewhere to go.

I have just returned from a site visit where I discovered that the contractor — a man I have worked with before, a man who should know better — has installed the wall panelling with visible seams that do not align with the ceiling grid. The panels are three millimetres off. Three millimetres. And he looked at me as though I were being unreasonable for noticing.

Three millimetres on wall panelling alignment is absolutely noticeable, especially at a ceiling junction where the eye naturally follows the line. That's not perfectionism — that's a legitimate defect in finish carpentry. What material are the panels?

Walnut veneer on MDF. Beautifully sourced walnut — I selected every sheet personally for grain continuity. And now the grain reads correctly but the panel edges do not meet the ceiling shadow gap at a consistent line. It creates a visual stutter that destroys the entire wall. I specified a two-millimetre shadow gap with zero tolerance on alignment. Zero. Not three-millimetre tolerance. Zero.

With grain-matched walnut veneer, that kind of misalignment is especially damaging because the eye is already tracking the grain pattern — any break in the geometry above it registers immediately. A shadow gap detail is specifically designed to create a precise, clean termination line. If the panels aren't hitting that line consistently, the shadow gap actually emphasises the error rather than concealing it. Is this something that can be corrected in place, or do the panels need to come off?

They need to come off. Three of the eight panels must be removed, the substrate shimmed, and the panels refitted. And here is what makes my blood run cold — he was about to proceed to the adjacent wall. If I had not visited today, the entire room would have been finished with this error, and I would have discovered it at final walkthrough with the client standing beside me. That is not a scenario I am willing to imagine.

Catching it before it propagated to the rest of the room is the saving grace here. But the fact that the crew was going to continue without flagging the misalignment — that's a process failure, not just a craftsmanship one. It means either they didn't check against the drawings, or they checked and decided three millimetres wasn't worth mentioning. Both are problems. How are you handling it with the contractor?

I spoke to him directly on site. I do not raise my voice — I have never found volume useful — but I made it exceptionally clear that this is a pattern I will not tolerate. This is not the first time I have found discrepancies on his work that required intervention. Last month it was a door frame that was plumb but not aligned to the adjacent wall reveal. The month before, skirting boards with inconsistent mitre joints. Each time he tells me the variation is within industry standard. And each time I tell him the same thing: I do not work to industry standard. I work to mine.

"I do not work to industry standard, I work to mine" is a perfectly reasonable position for a designer whose reputation depends on flawless execution. But the recurring pattern is concerning — you're essentially acting as your own quality control on every detail because you can't rely on the contractor to self-inspect to your standard. That's not sustainable. Have you considered a more formal quality control protocol with him, or is this reaching the point where you need a different contractor?

It is reaching that point, and it pains me because his team's base craftsmanship is good. Their joinery is clean, their material handling is careful. The problem is attention to tolerance at the detail level — the last five percent where good becomes excellent. He builds to a construction standard, not a design standard. And the gap between those two things is exactly where my work lives.

That's a precise diagnosis. The base skills are there but the finishing mentality isn't. Some contractors can be trained into that last five percent if you invest in the relationship — detailed inspection checklists, requiring self-check photos before you visit, making sign-off on each element mandatory before moving to the next. Others simply operate at a different level of care and no amount of documentation will change that. Given that you've already had multiple conversations about this, what's your instinct?

My instinct is to give him one more project with a formal protocol — the checklist approach you mentioned — and see whether the quality improves when the expectations are documented at every stage rather than simply specified in the drawings. If the same issues recur, I will find someone else. I owe it to the working relationship to try. But I will not sacrifice my standards for loyalty. The work must be impeccable. There is no room in my practice for almost.

That's a fair and structured approach — one more chance with clear documentation, measurable checkpoints, and defined consequences. For the checklist, I'd suggest breaking each element into stages with sign-off gates: substrate preparation (check and photograph), panel dry-fit (check alignment before fixing), first panel installed (verify before continuing), and full wall completion (final review). Each gate requires a photo submission and your approval before proceeding. That way issues are caught at the earliest possible moment, not after eight panels are up.

Yes. Stage gates. That is the correct framework. I will draft it this week and present it to him as the condition for continuing to work together. If he resists the process, that tells me everything I need to know. A craftsman who objects to quality control is not a craftsman I want on my sites. Thank you — this conversation has helped me channel the fury into something productive.

Channelling it into a systematic solution is the best outcome. And you're right — his response to the protocol itself will be diagnostic. A contractor who welcomes accountability is one worth investing in. I hope the walnut panels come out cleanly on the rework. Good luck with the conversation.
